original_abstract:
This paper examines the prevalence and determinants of maternal morbidity in Kenya, a critical but often overlooked dimension of reproductive health in Sub-Saharan Africa. While maternal mortality receives significant scholarly and policy attention, morbidity remains under-researched due to the scarcity of clinical data and the absence of robust vital registration systems. Utilizing data from the 1998 Kenya Demographic and Health Survey (DHS)—the only national survey in Kenya to collect detailed information on delivery complications—this study analyzes women’s self-reports of four major obstetric complications: prolonged labor, excessive bleeding, high fever with foul-smelling discharge, and convulsions.

The findings reveal that more than half of the respondents reported at least one complication, with prolonged labor and hemorrhage identified as the most frequent morbidities. The analysis demonstrates that reports of maternal morbidity are significantly influenced by parity, ethnicity, and the type of assistance received during delivery, alongside notable regional variations. Beyond the empirical analysis, the paper critically evaluates the methodological strengths and limitations of using self-reported data versus clinical observations in developing contexts. It addresses potential biases, such as recall accuracy and selection bias (near-miss survivors), yet concludes that in settings with high home-delivery rates and weak infrastructure, population-based self-reports currently offer the most viable means for estimating the national burden of maternal illness and informing maternal healthcare priorities.
humanized_abstract:
Maternal Morbidity in Kenya: An Assessment of Prevalence and Determinants This paper examines the prevalence and determinants of maternal morbidity in Kenya. Maternal mortality has received a disproportionate share of research attention and importance in the reproductive health literature, particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa. Maternal morbidity, the consequences of this attention, is poorly understood because of the scarcity of clinical data and the poor vital registration systems of countries in this region. This paper uses data from the 1998 Kenya Demographic and Health Survey (DHS). This is the only national survey in Kenya that has collected information on labour complications; the paper uses information on women's reports of four major obstetric complications: prolonged labour, heavy or severe bleeding, or high fever with purulent discharge, or convulsions.

According to the research findings, more than half of the respondents suffered from at least one complication after giving birth, with prolonged labour and postpartum haemorrhage being the most reported complications. The results show that self-reported maternal morbidity was significantly associated with primiparity, ethnicity and level of skill at the birth, and that there was considerable variation between different geographical areas. The paper offers a methodology critique of using self-reported data and clinical information to assess maternal morbidity in developing countries and discusses sources of bias including recall bias and selection bias (near-miss survivors). However, the prevalence of home births in developing countries and the lack of reliable health infrastructure suggest that population-based self-reported information is an appropriate tool to give an estimate of the national burden of maternal morbidity and to guide national maternal health priorities.
